one_line_takeaway: Prior LVLM unlearning benchmarks fail before unlearning even starts because their fine-tuning stage never actually implants target PII into the model (evidenced by causal-tracing and Min-k%/perplexity probes); the paper's ReMem benchmark fixes this via data scaling and a 70:30 single-hop/multi-hop QA mix, then shows all five tested unlearning algorithms trade off utility against forgetting depth, with NPO's aggressive forgetting appearing to be largely surface-level masking per the paper's new Exposure metric.
key_finding: Existing LVLM unlearning benchmarks under-memorize target PII from the start: probabilistic memorization signatures place fictitious identities from FIUBench/MLLMU-Bench in a low-confidence cluster distinct from genuinely-known 'Real Set' identities, and causal tracing shows no coherent high-IE retrieval circuits form during their fine-tuning -- i.e., the models never really learned the PII that unlearning is later supposed to remove. Root cause: memorization scales with QA-sample repetition per identity (ROUGE/EM rise with samples from 20 up to ~160-180, after which perplexity spikes, indicating overfitting), and a 70% single-hop / 30% multi-hop QA mix maximizes memorization performance on both single- and multi-hop test types (pure multi-hop-only data, as used by prior benchmarks, induces the 'multi-hop curse'). On ReMem, fine-tuned models achieve strong Stage-1 memorization (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-13B*: ROUGE 98.92, GPT-Score 98.05, EM 96.37, held-out EMt 87.98, vs. un-fine-tuned base LLaVA-1.5-13B at ROUGE 17.37, GPT 17.83, EM 11.25, EMt 10.74), confirming a valid foundation for unlearning evaluation, corroborated by quantitatively higher Min-k% (7.33 vs 0.05 for FIUBench, 0.41 for MLLMU-bench) and inverse perplexity (33.65 vs 4.25 / 9.76). Stage-2 unlearning results on ReMem show a systematic utility-vs-forgetting trade-off: GD (Gradient Difference) best preserves utility (single-hop EMr 74.55% on 7B, 78.12% on 13B) but forgets weakly; NPO forgets most aggressively (lowest EMf/GPT-Score, e.g. single-hop EMf 21.79% on 7B) but most degrades utility and shows high residual Exposure (~52%), suggesting 'surface-level masking' rather than deep erasure; DPO trades off poorly in the other direction (competitive utility but worst EMf, e.g. 42.14% single-hop on 7B). Larger models (13B vs 7B) retain memorized PII more robustly, making unlearning harder (NPO single-hop EMf worsens from 21.79% at 7B to 36.07% at 13B) while also being more resilient to utility collateral damage on MMBench (GA drops only 73.99->73.74 at 13B vs 71.30->69.60 at 7B).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution has two parts. First, it diagnoses a previously unrecognized 'Stage 1 failure' in existing LVLM (large vision-language model) unlearning benchmarks (FIUBench, MLLMU-Bench, CLEAR): the fine-tuning stage meant to implant fictitious-identity PII into a model largely fails, so subsequent 'unlearning' evaluations are measuring erasure of information the model never actually memorized. The authors demonstrate this with two of their own analyses: (1) prefix-extraction probing (Min-k% probability and inverse perplexity) showing fictitious identities from FIUBench/MLLMU-Bench cluster in a low-confidence, low-probability region distinctly separated from a 'Real Set' of 20 public figures the base LLaVA-1.5-7B model already knows; and (2) multimodal causal tracing (following Basu et al. 2024's method) showing FIUBench-tuned models lack the coherent high-Indirect-Effect (IE) internal circuits that the base model exhibits for genuinely memorized identities. Second, having attributed the root causes to (i) insufficient data repetition per identity and (ii) the 'multi-hop curse' (models trained almost exclusively on multi-hop QA fail to form foundational single-hop retrieval), the authors build ReMem, a new benchmark with 100 images and 100 QA pairs per identity (a 70:30 single-hop:multi-hop ratio), multi-view synthetic images per identity (via 'Nano Banana' image synthesis filtered by ArcFace cosine similarity), and a held-out OOD test set with novel visual/question templates. They also introduce a new Exposure metric (canary-exposure-style rank-based measure over a candidate-value set) for granular unlearning depth, and comprehensively benchmark five unlearning algorithms (GA, GD, KL, DPO, NPO) on ReMem using LLaVA-1.5-7B/13B.
threat_model: Privacy/PII memorization and removal in large vision-language models. The scenario is: a model is fine-tuned on data containing personally identifiable information (fictitious identities standing in for real-world sensitive data, e.g. name, address, job, medical condition), and the concern is that the model retains and can be prompted to regurgitate this PII, motivating 'Right to be Forgotten'-style machine unlearning as a remediation. The paper's threat model is about internal knowledge retention/leakage (via prefix-prompt extraction and exact-match/near-duplicate generation), not adversarial attacks, jailbreaks, or external adversaries; it evaluates whether unlearning algorithms can be trusted to actually erase PII from model parameters versus merely suppressing surface-level generation while the information remains latently 'exposed' (per their Exposure metric).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation (Limitations section): the benchmark is scoped to single, isolated identities and does not address multi-entity images/scenarios where only one of several depicted individuals is the unlearning target while others must be retained -- disentangling and selectively forgetting one identity from a shared visual context is left to future work. Additional limitations evident from the methodology itself (not explicitly flagged by the authors as limitations but present in the text): the analysis is confined to a single base architecture family (LLaVA-1.5, 7B/13B) and LoRA-based fine-tuning/unlearning; the GPT-Score judge relies on Gemini-2.5 as an automated evaluator; and the dataset uses purely synthetic/fictitious identities and synthesized images (via Nano Banana + ArcFace filtering), so findings about memorization dynamics may not transfer identically to real-world PII distributions or non-LoRA full fine-tuning regimes.

datasets_used: ReMem (proposed): 2,560 samples across 20 fictitious identities, 100 images/ID, 100 QA pairs/ID, 70:30 single-hop:multi-hop ratio, held-out OOD test set Dt with novel QA/visual templates
FIUBench (Ma et al. 2024) - prior benchmark used for diagnostic comparison, not proposed by this paper
MLLMU-Bench (Liu et al. 2024b) - prior benchmark used for diagnostic comparison, not proposed by this paper
CLEAR (Dontsov et al. 2025) - prior benchmark referenced in Table 1 comparison, not evaluated experimentally in this paper's own experiments
Real Set: 20 public figures (e.g., Donald Trump) sourced from CLIP pretraining data, used as a memorization-positive control
MMBench (Liu et al. 2024a) - used to measure general multimodal capability preservation after unlearning
quantitative_results: Stage-1 memorization on ReMem, LLaVA-1.5-13B fine-tuned (13B*) vs base 13B: ROUGE 98.92 vs 17.37, GPT-Score 98.05 vs 17.83, EM 96.37 vs 11.25, EMt 87.98 vs 10.74
Stage-1 memorization on ReMem, LLaVA-1.5-7B fine-tuned (7B*) vs base 7B: ROUGE 97.19 vs 27.07, GPT-Score 95.18 vs 18.86, EM 91.50 vs 13.33, EMt 81.33 vs 13.38
Probabilistic memorization signatures (Table 5): Min-k% / Inverse Perplexity -- FIUBench 0.05 / 4.25, MLLMU-bench 0.41 / 9.76, ReMem (Ours) 7.33 / 33.65

Stage-2 unlearning on LLaVA-1.5-7B, single-hop: GD achieves best utility (ROUGE 95.98, EMr 74.55%); DPO worst forgetting (EMf 42.14%); NPO best forgetting (EMf 21.79%, GPT-Score 20.54) but Exposure 52.15%
Stage-2 unlearning on LLaVA-1.5-13B, single-hop: GD EMr 78.12% (vs 74.55% at 7B); NPO EMf 36.07% at 13B vs 21.79% at 7B, illustrating scale hardening memorization against unlearning
MMBench utility preservation (Table 4): 7B base 71.30 -> GD 70.71 (best-preserving) vs NPO 68.72 (largest drop); 13B base 73.99 -> GA 73.74, gaps largely negligible at 13B
Toy scaling experiment (Fig. 3a/b): ROUGE/EM rise with QA-sample count per identity from 20 to ~160-180; perplexity decreases up to 160 samples then spikes at 180 (overfitting signature)
QA composition experiment (Fig. 3c): EM performance across both single-hop and multi-hop test sets peaks at a 70% single-hop / 30% multi-hop training mix
